key_points: Developer-focused breakdown: confirmed facts vs. speculation about GPT-6
Agentic tasks create unpredictable token spend (50K-500K per request internally)
Expected API pricing band: $2-$5 input / $2-$25 output per million tokens
2M context window speculation: 2x+ cost multiplier for extended context requests
Key advice: avoid hardcoding model IDs — use config so GPT-6 is a one-line switch
7-step migration checklist for GPT model upgrades, applicable to GPT-6 prep
Step 1: abstract model ID behind environment variable or config object
Step 2: build fallback routing to handle 429s during high-demand launch windows
Step 3: run domain-specific evaluation tests before switching production traffic
Staged rollout by traffic percentage is the recommended deployment pattern
Long-term memory: recall of preferences, projects, conversations across weeks/months
Agentic capabilities: better goal decomposition, more tool integrations, greater autonomy
Personalization: users can customize GPT-6 responses across sessions
Multimodal reasoning: potentially continuous video processing
Implication: stop injecting full conversation history — memory API will handle it
Memory reduces redundant context — eliminates re-sending conversation history every API call
Full codebase-level context for coding tasks, multi-file production-ready code from plain English
GPT-6 uses reinforcement learning as core training — can discover new algorithms
Business use cases: customer support with persistent context, predictive analytics
Two-tier inference (System-1 fast + System-2 verification) reduces hallucination to <0.1%
GPT-5.5 (Spud) complete developer guide — the current bridge model before GPT-6
GPT-5.5 API model IDs: gpt-5.5 and gpt-5.5-pro, available April 24, 2026
Responses API is the recommended interface — Assistants API being sunset in 2026
Structured Outputs with JSON Schema preferred over prompt-based output instructions
Tool calls should use strict: true for reliability gains without token overhead

stats.fact: Spud (codename) shipped as GPT-5.5 on April 23, 2026 — not GPT-6; real GPT-6 is now the next-next model
GPT-5.5 API pricing: $5 per 1M input tokens, $30 per 1M output tokens — exactly double GPT-5.4 pricing
GPT-5.5 achieved Intelligence Index score of 60, topping all 153 reasoning models on Artificial Analysis leaderboard (April 2026)
Polymarket traders price GPT-6 at 84% odds by December 31, 2026; ~45% by June 30, 2026
GPT-6 expected context window: 2 million tokens — double GPT-5.5's 1M token window
GPT-6 expected to deliver 40% performance improvement in coding, reasoning, and agentic tasks over current models
Stargate data center in Abilene, Texas completed GPT-6 pre-training on March 24, 2026 using 100,000+ H100/B200 GPUs
GPT-6 hallucination rate claimed below 0.1% with two-tier System-1/System-2 inference architecture
Smart model routing (GPT-6 for complex tasks, cheaper models for simple ones) saves 40-60% on total API spend
OpenAI Assistants API sunset scheduled for 2026; Responses API is the migration target for all agent workloads

angles: The Spud confusion explained: why the model developers waited for shipped as GPT-5.5 — and what GPT-6 actually means now for API planning
GPT-6 API preparation checklist: 7 concrete code changes to make today so GPT-6 is a one-config-line upgrade, not a rewrite
Memory-first architecture: how GPT-6's persistent memory changes the API call pattern — stop injecting full conversation history on every request
Agentic token economics: GPT-6 can burn 50K–500K tokens per agentic request — how to budget, cap, and route to avoid bill shock
Responses API migration: the Assistants API is being sunset in 2026 — developers who haven't migrated yet will be forced to when GPT-6 drops
Model routing strategy: not every task needs GPT-6 — route classification and simple tasks to GPT-5.2/DeepSeek and reserve GPT-6 for complex reasoning to cut costs 40-60%
GPT-6 vs GPT-5.5 for developers: what new API capabilities unlock (2M context, native memory, multimodal unification) vs. what stays the same
outline: H2: What Is GPT-6 (Spud)? Understanding the Naming Confusion
H2: GPT-6 Timeline: When Will the API Be Available?
H2: GPT-6 Expected API Capabilities: What Changes for Developers
H2: Step 1 — Abstract Your Model ID Behind Config (Do This Today)
H2: Step 2 — Migrate from Assistants API to Responses API Before GPT-6 Drops
H2: Step 3 — Build Memory-Aware Workflows to Exploit GPT-6's Persistent Memory
H2: Step 4 — Design an Agentic Token Budget and Fallback Strategy
H2: Step 5 — Set Up Model Routing: GPT-6 for Complex Tasks, Cheaper Models for Simple Ones
H2: Step 6 — Build Your GPT-6 Evaluation Dataset Now
H2: Step 7 — Plan a Staged Rollout with Feature Flags
H2: GPT-6 API Pricing Estimate and Cost Modeling
H2: GPT-6 Preparation Checklist: Everything in One Place
